# How the Compiler Works

## How the Compiler Works

What happens when you press "build" in your IDE? The compiler transforms your source code through several distinct stages into an executable program. Understanding this process helps you debug faster and write better C++ code.

::: notes
Total slides: 11
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The Compilation Process Overview

The path from source code to a running program involves three major stages: preprocessing, compilation, and linking. Each stage processes your code in a specific way and produces intermediate output. Together, they transform human-readable C++ into machine-executable code.

```{.plantuml}
@startuml compilation_stages

skinparam backgroundColor #FFFFFF
skinparam defaultFontName "Helvetica Neue"
skinparam defaultFontSize 13

skinparam rectangle {
  BackgroundColor #2C3E50
  FontColor #ECEFF1
  BorderColor #1A252F
  BorderThickness 2
  RoundCorner 14
  FontSize 13
}

skinparam note {
  BackgroundColor #F2F3F4
  BorderColor #BDC3C7
  FontColor #2C3E50
  FontSize 11
}

skinparam arrow {
  Color #7F8C8D
  FontColor #5D6D7E
  FontSize 10
  Thickness 2
}

left to right direction

note as N0
  <b>main.cpp</b>
  <i>.h files</i>
end note

rectangle "<b>1 — Preprocessor</b>\n\n&#35;include, &#35;define\nmacro expansion" as P

note as N1
  <b>Expanded Source</b>
  <i>(.ii)</i>
end note

rectangle "<b>2 — Compiler</b>\n\nsyntax & type check\n→ object code" as C

note as N2
  <b>Object File</b>
  <i>(.o)</i>
end note

rectangle "<b>3 — Linker</b>\n\nresolve symbols\ncombine .o files" as L

note as N3
  <b>Executable</b>
end note

note as LIB
  Standard Library
  <i>(.a / .so)</i>
end note

N0 --> P
P --> N1
N1 --> C
C --> N2
N2 --> L
L --> N3
LIB -up-> L

@enduml
```

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Stage 1: Preprocessing

The preprocessor runs before actual compilation and handles directives that start with `#`. It expands macros, inserts file contents from `#include` statements, and removes comments. The output is still C++ source code — just with all directives resolved.

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Preprocessing Directives

- `#include <file>` — inserts a header file's contents verbatim
- `#define NAME value` — textual substitution before compilation
- `#pragma once` / header guards — prevents duplicate inclusion

::: notes
`#include <file>`: The preprocessor inserts the entire contents of the header file into your source. It does not validate syntax — that happens in the compiler.

`#define NAME value`: Creates a textual substitution. Every occurrence of NAME is replaced with value before the compiler ever sees your code.

`#pragma once` (or header guards): Prevents a header from being inserted more than once per translation unit. Without it, including the same header twice causes redeclaration errors.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Stage 2: Compilation

The compiler takes preprocessed source code and performs syntax checking to verify it follows C++ grammar rules. It then translates valid code into assembly language or object code specific to your target processor. A single `.cpp` file becomes one `.o` (object) file.

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## What the Compiler Checks

- **Syntax**: Does your code follow C++ grammar rules?
- **Types**: Do variable types match in assignments, function calls, and operations? C++ is statically typed — type mismatches are caught here, not at runtime.
- **Scope and declarations**: Are variables and functions declared before use? Do names conflict?

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Stage 3: Linking

The linker takes multiple object files and combines them into one executable. It also resolves references to library functions. If a function is called but not defined anywhere, the linker reports an "undefined reference" error.

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Linking: How It Works

- **Symbol resolution**: The linker matches each function call to its definition across all object files and libraries.
- **Library linking**: Standard headers like `<iostream>` declare functions; the linker connects your calls to their implementations in the standard library.
- If `math.o` is missing, linking fails with "undefined reference to add()" even though `main.cpp` compiled cleanly.

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Translation Units

Each `.cpp` file is compiled independently — it is called a **translation unit**. The compiler sees only one `.cpp` file at a time; it has no knowledge of other `.cpp` files until the linker joins them. This is why declarations go in `.h` files: the preprocessor copies them into every `.cpp` that needs them.

```cpp
// math.h — declaration only (shared via #include)
int add(int a, int b);

// math.cpp — definition lives in its own translation unit
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

// main.cpp — sees the declaration via #include, not the definition
#include "math.h"
int main() { return add(2, 3); }
```

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## Name Mangling

C++ encodes each function's parameter types into its linker symbol name. This is called **name mangling** and is what makes function overloading possible at the linker level.

```cpp
int  add(int a, int b);     // mangled to something like: _Z3addii
double add(double a, double b); // mangled to: _Z3adddd
```

When you see a linker error like `undefined reference to '_Z3addii'`, the garbled name is the mangled form of `add(int, int)`. This is also why calling a C library from C++ requires `extern "C"` — C has no name mangling and the linker cannot match the symbols otherwise.

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## Summary

The compiler transforms source code through preprocessing, compilation, and linking. Understanding these stages helps you interpret error messages and locate bugs faster.

<!-- Slide 11 -->
